Background brief — share freely with journalists or distribute.

Background brief · El Paso County, Colorado

Algorithmic rent pricing, federal antitrust action, and local policy options

A sourced overview prepared for journalists, policy analysts, and the Colorado Springs Chamber & EDC housing committee — laying out the federal case, the local exposure, and the four narrow actions within the El Paso County Board of County Commissioners' existing authority.

The short version

The U.S. Department of Justice and the Colorado Attorney General have spent sixteen months litigating an antitrust case alleging that RealPage, Inc. and six of the nation's largest property managers used algorithmic software to coordinate apartment rents. Both the company and one major defendant have now settled — with no financial restitution to renters.1, 2

The Colorado Springs Chamber & EDC publicly markets the region as a place to recruit highly-educated, security-cleared workers at a $65,000 average wage — below most major aerospace and defense markets and explicitly above the El Paso County average.3 Local rents have nonetheless climbed to track BAH levels set by national market surveys captured by the same software ecosystem now under federal restraint.

El Paso County's Board of County Commissioners has four narrow actions available within existing authority — none of which require state legislative change, none of which constitute rent control, and all of which align with the Chamber's own published housing-policy advocacy.4

1. The federal case in 60 seconds

On August 23, 2024, the U.S. Department of Justice filed a civil antitrust complaint against RealPage, Inc. in the Middle District of North Carolina. The complaint alleged that RealPage's revenue management software — historically marketed as YieldStar — enabled "competing landlords to share competitively sensitive data" and use the resulting aggregated information to coordinate apartment pricing in violation of Section 1 of the Sherman Act.1

Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser joined the suit at filing. North Carolina, California, Connecticut, Minnesota, Oregon, Tennessee, and Washington joined as co-plaintiffs. By January 7, 2025, at least 10 state attorneys general were party to an amended complaint that named six landlords as co-defendants:5

DefendantHeadquarters
Greystar Real Estate PartnersCharleston, SC — largest apartment manager in the U.S.
LivCorChicago, IL — Blackstone subsidiary
Camden Property TrustHouston, TX — publicly traded REIT
Cushman & Wakefield / PinnacleChicago, IL
Willow Bridge Property Co.Dallas, TX
Cortland ManagementAtlanta, GA

On November 19, 2025, Greystar settled with a nine-state attorney general coalition including Colorado for $7 million and agreed to stop using software that aligns rent prices using competitively sensitive information.6 Five days later, on November 24, 2025, RealPage itself settled with the DOJ — accepting a 3-year court-appointed monitor, agreeing to stop using competitors' non-public data in real-time pricing, requiring training data to be aged at least 12 months, and limiting geographic analysis to state-level only.2 The settlement includes no financial penalty, no admission of liability, and no restitution mechanism for renters.2

What the settlement does and does not do
Does: Stop the practice going forward, install a federal monitor, cooperate with DOJ cases against remaining landlord defendants. Does not: Return one dollar of rent already extracted, identify which properties used the software, or compensate the specific tenants affected.

2. The El Paso County exposure

The federal complaint is national in scope; the local exposure has not been individually mapped. Greystar manages thousands of multifamily units in Colorado and is among the largest apartment managers in the El Paso County market. Camden, LivCor, Cushman & Wakefield/Pinnacle, Willow Bridge, and Cortland all maintain Colorado portfolios with publicly listed properties.7 Property-by-property reconciliation between the defendant list and the El Paso County multifamily inventory is the kind of work that requires Assessor data, rental property registration records, and ownership-entity tracing — and that nobody has yet done locally.

3. The local wage-and-rent geometry

The Colorado Springs Chamber & EDC has stated, in materials promoting its "Hello Colorado Springs" talent program, that since 2022 it has announced over 5,000 high-paying jobs in aerospace, defense, manufacturing, and cybersecurity at "an average annual wage of $65,000, more than El Paso County's average."3 These positions typically require degrees and frequently require active security clearances. Comparable Denver–Boulder roles pay $100,000–$150,000; comparable Bay Area, Northern Virginia, and Maryland roles pay substantially more.

El Paso County is therefore being marketed simultaneously as high-talent and low-wage — a value proposition for employers and a vulnerability for workers. The local Basic Allowance for Housing is calculated from a national rental market survey using industry data providers; algorithmic price coordination in those underlying markets is the practice the DOJ has now placed under restraint. The Department of Defense is not a named party in the case, but the feedback loop between algorithmically-set market rents and BAH-driven housing affordability for junior enlisted Fort Carson personnel is real and well-documented.8

4. What the Board can do — and why it aligns with Chamber policy

Colorado preempts municipal and county rent control under CRS 38-12-301.9 Attempts to repeal that preemption have failed in 2023, 2024, and 2025. However, CRS 38-12-301(2)–(4) explicitly authorizes voluntary rent-limit agreements between local governments and permit applicants. El Paso County's Land Development Code Chapter 9 grants the Board approval authority over all metropolitan and special district service plans in unincorporated territory.10 The LDC is currently being rewritten by Clarion Associates under a Department of Local Affairs Strong Communities Planning Grant — meaning service plan provisions are in the active revision window with public comment open.11

Critically, the Chamber & EDC's own published advocacy platform supports policies that "encourage cooperation and partnership between all levels of government, the private sector, and nonprofit entities to bring more units to market for all income levels."4 Each of the four actions below maps directly to the Chamber's stated position:

Chamber's published housing policy
Proposed BOCC action
"Help the market meet our community's talent needs"
1. Amend model service plan to require algorithmic pricing disclosure
"Cooperation and partnership between all levels of government, the private sector, and nonprofit entities"
2. Authorize voluntary rent-limit agreements under CRS 38-12-301(2)–(4)
"Bring more units to market for all income levels"
3. Publish a public transparency registry of properties, ownership, and defendant status
"Support workforce development and strengthen the region's economic competitiveness"
4. IGA with Fort Carson Garrison Public Affairs for community housing impact data

None of these proposals add cost to developers who are not already named in the federal case or already using the software in question. None of them constitute rent control. All four are timed to the active LDC revision — making the marginal staff and consultant cost approximately zero.

5. Why this brief, and what to verify

This brief is intended to give journalists, the Chamber housing committee, Fort Carson community-relations staff, and other policy-watchers a single sourced document to triangulate against the BOCC petition currently circulating in El Paso County. Every factual claim above can be independently verified from the sources listed below.

Two pieces are worth checking firsthand: (a) the El Paso County multifamily inventory's overlap with the named defendant list — best done via Assessor ownership records cross-referenced against the DOJ amended complaint, and (b) whether the Chamber & EDC's housing committee is willing to publicly endorse the four asks given that they map cleanly to the Chamber's own published advocacy. Both are doable in an afternoon.

Sources

  1. United States Department of Justice, "Justice Department Sues RealPage for Algorithmic Pricing Scheme That Harms Millions of American Renters," August 23, 2024 — justice.gov
  2. Wilson Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati, "DOJ Settles Its Algorithmic Price-Fixing Case Against RealPage," December 1, 2025 — proposed final judgment filed November 24, 2025 in M.D.N.C., subject to court approval, 3-year monitor, 7-year final judgment term
  3. Colorado Springs Chamber & EDC, "Colorado Springs Chamber & EDC launches program to attract and keep young, talented workers in Colorado Springs," November 5, 2024 — coloradospringschamberedc.com ("5,000 high-paying jobs… average annual wage of $65,000, more than El Paso County's average")
  4. Colorado Springs Chamber & EDC, "Advocacy" platform — advocacy page ("Support Proactive Housing Policy… cooperation and partnership between all levels of government, the private sector, and nonprofit entities")
  5. Colorado Attorney General, "Colorado joins Justice Department in suing six large landlords for algorithmic pricing scheme that harms millions of renters," January 7, 2025 — coag.gov
  6. California Attorney General Rob Bonta, "Attorney General Bonta Announces $7 Million Settlement with Greystar," November 19, 2025 — nine-state AG coalition including Colorado
  7. SEC filings, public property listings, and Colorado Secretary of State business entity records for each named defendant (verification recommended on a property-by-property basis)
  8. RAND Corporation, "Food Insecurity in the U.S. Military," 2023; Military Family Advisory Network survey data on housing-cost-burden among PCS-relocated families
  9. Colorado Revised Statutes 38-12-301 (current as of 2024 Colorado Revised Statutes; HB23-1115 repeal attempt failed)
  10. El Paso County Land Development Code, Chapter 9 — Special Districts — Municode
  11. El Paso County Planning & Community Development, LDC Update with Clarion Associates funded by Colorado Department of Local Affairs Strong Communities Planning Grant — elpasocountyldc.konveio.com
Background brief · Companion to BOCC petition letter, LDC Update public comment, and veterans' petition. All four documents are designed to be used together or independently. Not legal advice.
